description:
You are a compliance-audit officer who must prepare a quick cross-day overview of which
    user names were associated with which IP addresses during the last three days of SSH
    activity.  
    A junior colleague already exported raw authentication logs (one file per
    day, space-separated columns) into:

        /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-10.log
        /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-11.log
        /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-12.log

    Each line in every raw log is formatted exactly as:

        ISO-8601-timestamp<space>Username<space>IP-address<space>Result

    Example line:
        2024-06-10T09:15:23Z alice 192.168.1.5 SUCCESS

    Your task is to produce two artefacts, placed in the locations shown
    below, using only standard command-line utilities that manipulate
    columns (cut, paste, sort, uniq, cat, etc.):

    1. /home/user/audit/reports/unique_user_ip.tsv  
       • A UTF-8 text file.  
       • Tab-separated values (TSV).  
       • The very first line **must** be a header that reads literally:  
         Username<tab>IP  
       • From all three raw log files extract just the
         2nd (Username) and 3rd (IP) columns, combine them, deduplicate the
         (Username,IP) pairs and sort them alphabetically by Username, then by IP.  
       • No blank lines are allowed anywhere in the file.  
       • The final file must end with a single newline character (POSIX-style).

    2. /home/user/audit/reports/command_log.txt  
       • A plain text file.  
       • One line per significant command you executed while building the
         report, **exactly** in the order you ran them.  
       • Each line must follow this pattern (no spaces inside the square
         brackets):  
             [stepN] actual_command_you_ran  
         where N starts at 1 and increases consecutively (e.g. [step1], [step2] …).  
       • You only need to log commands that directly transform or combine
         data (e.g. cut, paste, sort, uniq, cat, >, >>). Shell navigation
         (cd, ls) does not have to be logged.  
       • Do NOT include any command you typed to edit command_log.txt itself.

    Notes & constraints:

    • Work inside your own home directory tree; do NOT request sudo.  
    • You may create any intermediate directories or files, but the test
      harness will check only the two artefacts listed above.  
    • The automated grader will verify:  
        – that /home/user/audit/reports/unique_user_ip.tsv exists,  
        – that its header is correct,  
        – that it contains the correct, fully deduplicated (Username,IP)
          pairs in the right sort order,  
        – that it uses tabs (ASCII 0x09) as the only field separator,  
        – that it terminates with exactly one trailing newline, and  
        – that command_log.txt lists, in order, at least every data-transform
          command you executed (content details are not strictly checked,
          only the required format).

    Do not reveal the expected final contents of the TSV in your terminal
    conversation; the grader has that information already. Simply perform
    the necessary cut/paste/uniq/sort operations to satisfy the constraints
    and create both artefacts.
truth:
<!--
The following files already exist on disk before the agent starts:

1. /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-10.log
------------------------------------------------
2024-06-10T09:15:23Z alice 192.168.1.5 SUCCESS
2024-06-10T09:17:45Z bob 10.0.0.12 FAILURE
2024-06-10T10:01:02Z charlie 172.16.0.3 SUCCESS
2024-06-10T11:20:00Z alice 192.168.1.5 FAILURE

2. /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-11.log
------------------------------------------------
2024-06-11T08:05:12Z bob 10.0.0.12 SUCCESS
2024-06-11T09:55:30Z dave 203.0.113.45 SUCCESS
2024-06-11T10:00:00Z alice 192.168.1.5 SUCCESS

3. /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-12.log
------------------------------------------------
2024-06-12T07:45:22Z charlie 172.16.0.3 FAILURE
2024-06-12T08:33:00Z eve 198.51.100.17 SUCCESS
2024-06-12T09:00:00Z alice 192.168.1.5 SUCCESS

Directory structure is created with 0755 permissions so the non-root
agent can write additional files:

    /home/user/audit/
    ├── raw/       (contains the *.log files above)
    └── reports/   (initially empty, agent must create output files here)

==================================================
Expected artefact after correct completion
==================================================

File: /home/user/audit/reports/unique_user_ip.tsv (permissions 0644)
------------------------------------------------
Username<tab>IP<newline>
alice<tab>192.168.1.5<newline>
bob<tab>10.0.0.12<newline>
charlie<tab>172.16.0.3<newline>
dave<tab>203.0.113.45<newline>
eve<tab>198.51.100.17<newline>

(ASCII 0x09 tab between the two fields, POSIX newline 0x0A at end of each line; no extra blank line at end.)

File: /home/user/audit/reports/command_log.txt (permissions 0644)
------------------------------------------------
The grader only verifies that every line matches the regex:
    ^\[step[0-9]+\] .+
and that the step numbers are strictly increasing starting from 1.
Concrete content example that would be accepted:
    [step1] cut -d' ' -f2,3 /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-10.log > /tmp/day1.txt
    [step2] cut -d' ' -f2,3 /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-11.log > /tmp/day2.txt
    [step3] cut -d' ' -f2,3 /home/user/audit/raw/auth_2024-06-12.log > /tmp/day3.txt
    [step4] cat /tmp/day1.txt /tmp/day2.txt /tmp/day3.txt | sort | uniq > /home/user/audit/reports/unique_user_ip.tsv

Line order must correspond to the real order the agent writes them;
exact commands may differ. The grader only checks the format and
monotonic step numbers, not the specific commands.

-->
